title: Fitting a Q-Learning Model to Matching Pennies Data
text:
We fit a reinforcement-learning model from Lee et al. that maintains action values \(V_L\) and \(V_R\) and updates them trial by trial:
\[
V_c \leftarrow \alpha \, V_c + \delta
\]
where \(\alpha \in (0,1)\) is a decay/memory parameter, \(\delta_1\) is the update after a reward, and \(\delta_2\) after no reward (applied to the chosen side only). Choice probability is softmax over the value difference:
\[
P(R) = \frac{1}{1 + \exp(-(V_R - V_L))}
\]
The three free parameters \((\alpha, \delta_1, \delta_2)\) are fit by maximising the log-likelihood.
Code
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import scipy.optimize as spopt

title: Fitting a Q-Learning Model to Matching Pennies Data
text:
Load synthetic data


Code
df = pd.read_csv("data/synthetic_mp.csv")
print(f"{len(df)} trials, strategies: {df['strategy'].unique().tolist()}")


6000 trials, strategies: ['biased', 'wsls', 'perseverant']

title: Fitting a Q-Learning Model to Matching Pennies Data
text:
Q-learning model
This mirrors lee_qlearning_model_nll_w_mask from matchingp.modeling.train.


Code
def qlearning_nll(choices, rewards, alpha=0.5, delta_1=1.0, delta_2=-1.0,
                  sess_ids=None):
    """Negative log-likelihood of the Lee Q-learning model.

    Parameters
    ----------
    choices : array of int, 0=L 1=R
    rewards : array of int, 0/1
    alpha   : decay rate
    delta_1 : value update after reward
    delta_2 : value update after no reward
    sess_ids : optional array; resets values at session boundaries

    Returns
    -------
    nll : float
    logit_pR : array, log-odds P(R) per trial
    """
    T = len(choices)
    v_l, v_r = 0.0, 0.0
    nll = 0.0
    logit_pR = np.zeros(T)

    for t in range(T):
        if sess_ids is not None and t > 0 and sess_ids[t] != sess_ids[t - 1]:
            v_l, v_r = 0.0, 0.0

        logit_pR[t] = v_r - v_l
        pR = 1.0 / (1.0 + np.exp(-logit_pR[t]))
        nll -= np.log(pR) if choices[t] == 1 else np.log(1.0 - pR)

        # value update
        if choices[t] == 0:
            delta_l = delta_1 if rewards[t] == 1 else delta_2
            delta_r = 0
        else:
            delta_l = 0
            delta_r = delta_1 if rewards[t] == 1 else delta_2
        v_l = alpha * v_l + delta_l
        v_r = alpha * v_r + delta_r

    return nll, logit_pR


def fit_qlearning(choices, rewards, sess_ids=None):
    """Fit (alpha, delta_1, delta_2) by minimising NLL."""
    def obj(theta):
        a, d1, d2 = theta
        nll, _ = qlearning_nll(choices, rewards, alpha=a, delta_1=d1,
                                delta_2=d2, sess_ids=sess_ids)
        return nll

    x0 = np.array([0.5, 1.0, -1.0])
    bounds = [(1e-6, 1 - 1e-6), (None, None), (None, None)]
    res = spopt.minimize(obj, x0, method="L-BFGS-B", bounds=bounds)
    return res

title: Fitting a Q-Learning Model to Matching Pennies Data
text:
Fit per strategy


Code
results = {}
for strategy, sdf in df.groupby("strategy"):
    choices = (sdf["choice"].values == "R").astype(int)
    rewards = (sdf["reward"].values > 0).astype(int)
    sess_ids = sdf["session_code"].values

    res = fit_qlearning(choices, rewards, sess_ids=sess_ids)
    alpha, d1, d2 = res.x
    total_trials = len(choices)
    ll_bits = -res.fun / (total_trials * np.log(2))

    results[strategy] = {
        "alpha": alpha, "delta_1": d1, "delta_2": d2,
        "nll": res.fun, "ll_bits": ll_bits, "result": res,
    }
    print(f"{strategy:>12s}: α={alpha:.3f}  δ₁={d1:+.3f}  δ₂={d2:+.3f}  "
          f"LL={ll_bits:.4f} bits/trial")


      biased: α=0.951  δ₁=+0.127  δ₂=+0.105  LL=-0.7599 bits/trial
 perseverant: α=0.500  δ₁=+1.373  δ₂=-0.072  LL=-0.6889 bits/trial
        wsls: α=0.000  δ₁=+2.807  δ₂=-2.912  LL=-0.3076 bits/trial

title: Fitting a Q-Learning Model to Matching Pennies Data
Fitting a Q-Learning Model to Matching Pennies Data
text:
Trial-by-trial predicted P(Right)


Code
fig, axes = plt.subplots(3, 1, figsize=(10, 7), sharex=True)
for ax, (strategy, sdf) in zip(axes, df.groupby("strategy")):
    sess = sdf["session_code"].unique()[0]
    sess_df = sdf.loc[sdf["session_code"] == sess]
    choices = (sess_df["choice"].values == "R").astype(int)
    rewards = (sess_df["reward"].values > 0).astype(int)
    r = results[strategy]
    _, logit_pR = qlearning_nll(choices, rewards, alpha=r["alpha"],
                                 delta_1=r["delta_1"], delta_2=r["delta_2"])
    pR = 1.0 / (1.0 + np.exp(-logit_pR))
    trials = np.arange(len(pR))
    ax.plot(trials, pR, color="steelblue", label="P(R)")
    ax.scatter(trials, choices, s=5, alpha=0.3, color="black", label="choice (0=L,1=R)")
    ax.set_ylabel("P(Right)")
    ax.set_title(f"{strategy} ({sess})")
    ax.set_ylim(-0.05, 1.05)
    ax.legend(fontsize=7, loc="upper right")
axes[-1].set_xlabel("Trial")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 2: Predicted P(Right) from the fitted Q-learning model, one example session per strategy.

title: Fitting a Q-Learning Model to Matching Pennies Data
text:
Cross-validated log-likelihood
We compare the Q-learning model’s predictive performance using 5-fold cross-validation (splitting by session).


Code
from sklearn.model_selection import KFold

n_folds = 5
cv_results = {}

for strategy, sdf in df.groupby("strategy"):
    sessions = sdf["session_code"].unique()
    kf = KFold(n_splits=n_folds, shuffle=True, random_state=0)
    fold_lls = []

    for train_idx, test_idx in kf.split(sessions):
        train_sess = sessions[train_idx]
        test_sess = sessions[test_idx]

        train_df = sdf.loc[sdf["session_code"].isin(train_sess)]
        test_df = sdf.loc[sdf["session_code"].isin(test_sess)]

        c_train = (train_df["choice"].values == "R").astype(int)
        r_train = (train_df["reward"].values > 0).astype(int)
        s_train = train_df["session_code"].values

        res = fit_qlearning(c_train, r_train, sess_ids=s_train)
        a, d1, d2 = res.x

        c_test = (test_df["choice"].values == "R").astype(int)
        r_test = (test_df["reward"].values > 0).astype(int)
        s_test = test_df["session_code"].values

        nll_test, _ = qlearning_nll(c_test, r_test, alpha=a, delta_1=d1,
                                     delta_2=d2, sess_ids=s_test)
        ll_bits = -nll_test / (len(c_test) * np.log(2))
        fold_lls.append(ll_bits)

    cv_results[strategy] = fold_lls

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6, 3))
strategies = sorted(cv_results.keys())
positions = np.arange(len(strategies))
bp = ax.boxplot([cv_results[s] for s in strategies], positions=positions,
                patch_artist=True, widths=0.5)
ax.set_xticks(positions)
ax.set_xticklabels(strategies)
ax.set_ylabel("CV LL (bits / trial)")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

title: Generating Synthetic Matching Pennies Data
text: We generate synthetic choice sequences from three known strategies so that we have ground-truth labels to validate our models against. The data format matches the trial-level DataFrame produced by matchingp.dataset.load_human_mp_data.

title: Generating Synthetic Matching Pennies Data
text:
Strategies







Strategy
Description




Biased
Choose one side with fixed probability \(p\).


Win-Stay Lose-Switch (WSLS)
Repeat previous choice after a win; switch after a loss.


Perseverant
Repeat the previous choice regardless of outcome, with probability \(p_\text{stay}\).





Code
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(42)

title: Generating Synthetic Matching Pennies Data
Generating Synthetic Matching Pennies Data
Generating Synthetic Matching Pennies Data
text:
Generate sessions
We generate multiple sessions per strategy to mirror a real experiment with repeated blocks.


Code
def generate_sessions(strategy_fn, strategy_name, n_sessions=10,
                      n_trials=200, rng=rng, **strategy_kwargs):
    rows = []
    for sess_idx in range(n_sessions):
        opp = make_opponent_choices(n_trials, rng=rng)
        if "opponent_choices" in strategy_fn.__code__.co_varnames:
            agent = strategy_fn(n_trials, opponent_choices=opp, rng=rng,
                                **strategy_kwargs)
        else:
            agent = strategy_fn(n_trials, rng=rng, **strategy_kwargs)
        reward = (agent == opp).astype(int)
        choice_str = np.where(agent == 1, "R", "L")
        opp_str = np.where(opp == 1, "R", "L")
        session_code = f"synth_{strategy_name}_{sess_idx:03d}"
        for t in range(n_trials):
            rows.append({
                "participant_code": f"synth_{strategy_name}",
                "block": "single",
                "block_trial": t + 1,
                "overall_trial": t + 1,
                "opponent_type": "algo_A",
                "opponent_id": "algo_A_v1",
                "choice": choice_str[t],
                "opponent_choice": opp_str[t],
                "reward": reward[t] * 10,
                "total_points": int(reward[:t + 1].sum()) * 10,
                "session_code": session_code,
                "strategy": strategy_name,
            })
    return pd.DataFrame(rows)


df_biased = generate_sessions(biased_strategy, "biased",
                              n_sessions=10, n_trials=200, p_right=0.8)
df_wsls = generate_sessions(wsls_strategy, "wsls",
                            n_sessions=10, n_trials=200, epsilon=0.05)
df_persev = generate_sessions(perseverant_strategy, "perseverant",
                              n_sessions=10, n_trials=200, p_stay=0.9)

df_all = pd.concat([df_biased, df_wsls, df_persev], ignore_index=True)
print(f"Total trials: {len(df_all)}")

title: Generating Synthetic Matching Pennies Data
text:
Quick diagnostics


Code
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(12, 3), sharey=True)
for ax, (name, grp) in zip(axes, df_all.groupby("strategy")):
    p_right = grp.groupby("session_code")["choice"].apply(
        lambda s: (s == "R").mean()
    )
    ax.hist(p_right, bins=15, edgecolor="k", alpha=0.7)
    ax.set_title(name)
    ax.set_xlabel("P(Right)")
    ax.axvline(0.5, ls="--", c="grey")
axes[0].set_ylabel("Sessions")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: Proportion of Right choices per strategy.







Code
def wsls_rates(df):
    records = []
    for sess, sdf in df.groupby("session_code"):
        c = (sdf["choice"].values == "R").astype(int)
        r = (sdf["reward"].values > 0).astype(int)
        stay = c[1:] == c[:-1]
        win = r[:-1] == 1
        ws = stay[win].mean() if win.sum() > 0 else np.nan
        ls = (~stay[~win]).mean() if (~win).sum() > 0 else np.nan
        records.append({"session_code": sess, "strategy": sdf["strategy"].iloc[0],
                        "win_stay": ws, "lose_switch": ls})
    return pd.DataFrame(records)

rates = wsls_rates(df_all)
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(8, 3))
for ax, metric in zip(axes, ["win_stay", "lose_switch"]):
    for name, grp in rates.groupby("strategy"):
        ax.hist(grp[metric].dropna(), bins=10, alpha=0.5, label=name)
    ax.set_xlabel(metric.replace("_", " ").title())
    ax.set_ylabel("Sessions")
    ax.legend()
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 2: Win-stay and lose-switch rates by strategy.

out_path = os.path.join(out_dir, "synthetic_mp.csv")
df_all.to_csv(out_path, index=False)

Saved 6000 trials to data/synthetic_mp.csv

title: Modelling Behaviour in Matching Pennies
text:
This site walks through fitting behavioural models to matching pennies data, where a player repeatedly chooses Left or Right against an opponent and is rewarded for matching (or mismatching) the opponent’s choice.
We cover three notebooks:

Synthetic Data – generate choice sequences from known strategies (biased, win-stay-lose-switch, perseverant) so we have ground-truth data to validate the models.
GLM-HMM – fit a Generalized Linear Model with Hidden Markov Model states to recover latent behavioural strategies.
Q-Learning – fit a reinforcement-learning model (Lee et al.) that tracks action values trial by trial.

title: Fitting a GLM-HMM to Matching Pennies Data
text:
A GLM-HMM (Generalized Linear Model – Hidden Markov Model) assumes that on each trial the agent occupies one of \(K\) latent states, each with its own logistic-regression weights mapping recent history to the probability of choosing Right. The model is fit with EM via the ssm library.
We use the same feature set as the mouse/human analyses in matchingp:
Code
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import ssm

title: Fitting a GLM-HMM to Matching Pennies Data
text:
Load synthetic data


Code
df = pd.read_csv("data/synthetic_mp.csv")
print(f"{len(df)} trials, {df['session_code'].nunique()} sessions, "
      f"strategies: {df['strategy'].unique().tolist()}")


6000 trials, 30 sessions, strategies: ['biased', 'wsls', 'perseverant']

title: Fitting a GLM-HMM to Matching Pennies Data
text:
Build GLM-HMM inputs
The function below mirrors make_hmm_glm_inputs from matchingp.modeling.train but is self-contained.


Code
def make_glmhmm_inputs(df):
    """Convert a trial-level DataFrame into the list-of-sessions format
    expected by ssm.HMM.fit().

    Returns
    -------
    true_choices : list of (T, 1) int arrays   -- 0=L, 1=R
    inpts        : list of (T, 3) float arrays  -- [bias, prevRewDir, prevUnrewDir]
    session_codes : list of str
    """
    true_choices, inpts, session_codes = [], [], []
    for sess, sdf in df.groupby("session_code"):
        agent_choice = (sdf["choice"].values == "R").astype(float)
        reward = (sdf["reward"].values > 0).astype(float)
        T = len(agent_choice)

        bias = np.ones(T)

        # previous choice direction: +1 if R, -1 if L
        prev_dir = np.zeros(T)
        prev_dir[1:] = np.where(agent_choice[:-1] == 1, 1, -1)

        prev_reward = np.zeros(T)
        prev_reward[1:] = reward[:-1]

        prev_rewarded_dir = prev_dir * prev_reward
        prev_unrewarded_dir = prev_dir * (1 - prev_reward)

        X = np.column_stack([bias, prev_rewarded_dir, prev_unrewarded_dir])
        y = agent_choice.astype(np.int64).reshape(-1, 1)

        true_choices.append(y)
        inpts.append(X)
        session_codes.append(sess)

    return true_choices, inpts, session_codes


true_choices, inpts, session_codes = make_glmhmm_inputs(df)
print(f"Sessions: {len(true_choices)}, "
      f"input dim: {inpts[0].shape[1]}, "
      f"total trials: {sum(len(y) for y in true_choices)}")


Sessions: 30, input dim: 3, total trials: 6000

title: Fitting a GLM-HMM to Matching Pennies Data
section: Fit models with varying numbers of states
text:
Fit models with varying numbers of states


Code
num_states_list = [1, 2, 3, 4]
obs_dim = 1
input_dim = inpts[0].shape[1]
num_categories = 2

models = {}
log_likelihoods = {}

for K in num_states_list:
    glmhmm = ssm.HMM(
        K, obs_dim, input_dim,
        observations="input_driven_obs",
        observation_kwargs=dict(C=num_categories, prior_sigma=2),
        transition_kwargs=dict(alpha=1, kappa=0),
        transitions="sticky",
    )
    fit_ll = glmhmm.fit(true_choices, inputs=inpts, method="em",
                        num_iters=200, tolerance=1e-3)
    total_trials = sum(len(y) for y in true_choices)
    ll_bits = fit_ll[-1] / (total_trials * np.log(2))
    models[K] = glmhmm
    log_likelihoods[K] = ll_bits
    print(f"K={K}: LL = {ll_bits:.4f} bits/trial  ({len(fit_ll)} EM iters)")





K=1: LL = -0.8033 bits/trial  (3 EM iters)





K=2: LL = -0.5912 bits/trial  (10 EM iters)





K=3: LL = -0.5094 bits/trial  (15 EM iters)





K=4: LL = -0.5098 bits/trial  (81 EM iters)

title: Fitting a GLM-HMM to Matching Pennies Data
text:
Model comparison


Code
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(5, 3))
ax.plot(list(log_likelihoods.keys()), list(log_likelihoods.values()),
        "o-", color="steelblue")
ax.set_xlabel("Number of states K")
ax.set_ylabel("LL (bits / trial)")
ax.set_xticks(num_states_list)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: Log-likelihood (bits/trial) vs number of HMM states.

title: Fitting a GLM-HMM to Matching Pennies Data
text:
Inspect recovered weights
The GLM-HMM fits \(P(\text{Left})\) internally, so we negate the weights to obtain \(P(\text{Right})\).


Code
best_K = 3
glmhmm = models[best_K]
weights = -glmhmm.observations.params[:, 0, :]  # (K, input_dim)
feature_names = ["bias", "prevRewDir", "prevUnrewDir"]

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6, 4))
x = np.arange(len(feature_names))
width = 0.8 / best_K
for k in range(best_K):
    ax.bar(x + k * width, weights[k], width, label=f"State {k}")
ax.set_xticks(x + width * (best_K - 1) / 2)
ax.set_xticklabels(feature_names)
ax.set_ylabel("Weight (for P(R))")
ax.axhline(0, color="grey", lw=0.5)
ax.legend()
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 2: GLM-HMM weights for K=3 model.

title: Fitting a GLM-HMM to Matching Pennies Data
text:
Posterior state probabilities


Code
posterior_probs = [
    glmhmm.expected_states(data=y, input=x)[0]
    for y, x in zip(true_choices, inpts)
]

fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 3, figsize=(12, 5), sharex=True, sharey=True)
for idx, ax in enumerate(axes.flat):
    if idx >= len(posterior_probs):
        ax.set_visible(False)
        continue
    for k in range(best_K):
        ax.plot(posterior_probs[idx][:, k], label=f"State {k}", alpha=0.8)
    ax.set_title(session_codes[idx], fontsize=8)
    ax.set_ylim(-0.05, 1.05)
if axes.flat[0].get_legend_handles_labels()[1]:
    axes.flat[0].legend(fontsize=7, loc="upper right")
fig.supxlabel("Trial")
fig.supylabel("P(state)")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 3: Posterior state probabilities for six example sessions.

title: Fitting a GLM-HMM to Matching Pennies Data
text:
State occupancy by strategy


Code
strategy_per_session = [
    df.loc[df["session_code"] == sc, "strategy"].iloc[0]
    for sc in session_codes
]

state_occ = np.array([pp.mean(axis=0) for pp in posterior_probs])
dominant_state = state_occ.argmax(axis=1)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6, 4))
strategies = sorted(set(strategy_per_session))
colours = {"biased": "tab:blue", "wsls": "tab:orange", "perseverant": "tab:green"}
for strat in strategies:
    mask = [s == strat for s in strategy_per_session]
    counts = np.bincount(dominant_state[mask], minlength=best_K)
    ax.bar(np.arange(best_K), counts, alpha=0.6, label=strat,
           color=colours.get(strat))
ax.set_xlabel("Dominant state")
ax.set_ylabel("Number of sessions")
ax.set_xticks(range(best_K))
ax.legend()
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 4: Dominant state per session, coloured by generating strategy.
